sentences.text: Yes.
That's correct-- Die Remuh Synagogue.
There is a story attached to it-- that people were celebrating wedding.
And because they did not stop before Shabbat came on Friday-- the wedding was held on Friday-- the synagogue collapsed, and all the people were killed.
So nobody has wedding on Friday.
And they certainly don't dance on Friday or Saturday.
Oh, just stones of very famous, old, very revered elders in that particular city.
And the rabbis were buried there.
And the synagogue exist to this day-- not the synagogue.
I'm referring to the cemetery.
That's where they are buried.
The synagogue is separately next to it-- tiny little synagogue-- to which I went in.
And, you know, each story I am telling you brings certain things to my mind.
When I was in Poland, in '89, I went to that synagogue.
I went to the cemetery on Saturday too, to bring back the-- the-- the moments that I was there with my mother.
And I saw somebody dressed differently.
Mostly they are old people-- thin, poorly dressed.
But there was one fellow, looking a little bit more like a Western Jew, who arrived there for a visit, like I did.
And I looked at him, and I spoke to the elder of the synagogue.
And I ask, who is that man?
And he said, oh, he is not from this synagogue.
He came from America.
I said, would you please go and ask him if his name is Max Hilfstein?
He went there, and he said, yes, this is Max Hilfstein.
It's most incredible, because he was then in his late 70s or 80s, and couldn't get over it that I have recognized him.
There was a good reason why I recognized him.
He lived in Podgorze.
He had a hairdressing salon.
And each day I was going to school, he was standing there with his blond curls.
And so I've seen him every day-- and, of course, unmistakably, that it was him.
Well, the fact that I have been so very sick-- I have been kept very much at home.
And there were all sorts of rumors.
In this same time, there were-- a lot of German Jews came to Krakow after-- whom the welfare society was looking after.
And my mother, again, was going there to help serve food to them.
And we heard all these stories, which we did not believe-- that the Germans could stoop to all that.
They were so civilized!
We were always believing that they just would fight the soldiers, but not the civilians.
And my mother would come home.
And she said, they told such-and-such a story-- whispering to my father-- and-- not our experience from first world war.
They're very civilized.
They won't do anything to us.
We came back.
We managed to sort of reconstruct our life a little bit.
And then I was allowed to go outside.
It was no question of going back to school.
Nobody went, not even Polish people.
I don't think the schools were resumed, because it was two months holiday.
In Poland, you have two months holiday during the summertime.
It was pretty normal.
There was no danger-- what we did here-- that somebody was shot somewhere, in Kazimierz, where the Jews were wearing beards, and peyos, and were dressing in those little long, black mantles.
Well, when you think-- somebody would say, why do they have to do it?
They should a little bit control themselves.
We felt that that's the reason why they were shot.
We wouldn't be shot.
They wouldn't do anything to us.
Came winter, they started to collect us to clean the snow in the streets-- never any payment, never food.
But we accept it.
We were glad to be able to be of some use, and work.
And then, all of a sudden, there came a announcement what we all will have to go into ghetto.
So, I think, in 1940, or '41-- end of '40, '41-- we went into ghetto.
And we were--
I did.
I did.
Yes, there was one specific incident which upset me and my mother very much.
I went to the station to buy food and to buy milk, because that's where the peasants were arriving with all the goods.
And I have met a German, whom, at that stage, I wasn't quite sure who he was.
But he was brother of my girlfriend from Henryk Sienkiewicz school.
His name was Elsner-- a young boy.
During the prewar times, he was sent to Germany.
He-- the whole family was volksdeutsche-- part German.
Because he had to go to jail, so the parents sent him off to Germany.
And in Germany he became a big shot-- return in uniform, with a gun.
He could not have been more than 19, 20.
No, he was not a policeman.
He belonged to some unit-- some German unit.
He was not SS.
He was not Gestapo.
He was just little somebody that got uniform and gun, and he was using it to enjoy it.
I really don't know what rank he held-- probably none.
He spotted me.
He must have known who I was.
I have always been taller than the rest, and it was very easy to recognize me.
And so was my father.
And because there were not many Jewish family living in that area, everybody knew that I was Schermant.
And, of course, Schermant was synonymous with being Jewish.
I was buying milk.
And he came to me, and in German told me, how dare you, Jude-- Jew.
You're buying milk here.
March!
So I got hold of him-- we were probably the same height-- by his lapels.
And I was much, much younger than him.
And I said to him, in Polish, how dare you, telling me I'm a Jew!
How dare you-- fighting with him.
Of course, he didn't let me win.
He grabbed that thing.
After all, he was a man-- spilled the milk, and was grabbing for his revolver.
Well, terrified-- terrified, I ran home, which was close by to the station, through the back streets.
But he managed to be in my house before me, because he didn't go through the back alleys.
He went straight to my home.
And my mother opened the door.
And he yelled, in German, that we are Jews.
And we must not go to station to buy food, and all, [GERMAN]-- to my mother.
Oh, it's a swear words-- devil should take you, and whatever.
And my mother, in-- equally in German, told him, now, what crime did she commit?
What is it that you want?
We were not totally afraid yet-- even seeing a young boy with a gun.
And that sort of disarm him, and he went away.
But from then on, I was terrified to go into the street, looking constantly if Elsner is not coming somewhere.
Because his parents also live in that area, and the sister whom I knew from school.
So, you know, that was a threat even from my parents.
They said, now, don't go into town.
Don't go out, because Elsner will get you.
Were you wearing armbands, at that point?
Not-- yes, we did.
We did.
But that was no problem, because I often used to take the armband, put in the pocket, and-- and run without it.
Rarely did I have the armband, outside.
And very shortly after, we were ushered into the ghetto.
And for me, it was almost a relief.
Now I felt more secure that Elsner won't get me, in-- in ghetto.
But even in ghetto, I used to go out and go to that railway station to buy food.
There was a policeman on the inside of the ghetto.
There was a--
Polish policeman-- and a soldier on the outside of the ghetto.
And part of it was a brick wall, and part of it was wires-- what's that-- bar-- barbed wire-- in places-- that we should not escape, should not cross.
And there was also a tram going through the ghetto, out of the ghetto, into the Polish sector, and back into the ghetto.
And then the other end of the ghetto was leaving and going into city.

And for me, that was a marvelous way-- while they had to stop for the gate to be open for the tram-- to hop on on the tram and get out of the ghetto, equally back, to hop onto the tram and get back into the ghetto, with whatever I managed to buy.
There was one particular conductor.
His name was Stefan Grabowski.
And he was extremely nice.
He would specially open that door, or leave little ajar, so I could push it off, get in-- in that moment where the German policeman was past.
And the Polish policemens were-- you know, they didn't really try to shoot or anything like that.
It was not that severely guarded.

And later on, I have devised a method of taking my kennkarte Ausweis, the things that-- like a pass-- and put some money in between, and open to identify myself to the Polish policeman, give him some money.
He took the money nicely, put it in his pocket, and winked to the German soldier to let me out.
I even had the audacity to ask, till what time are you on duty, so that I had access back to the ghetto.
Because I really felt there is nowhere for me to stay.
And it was dangerous.
But I think when one is so young, one doesn't stop to consider the danger one is in.
I often used to bring to ghetto-- I was the source of bringing potatoes, carrots, a bit-- whatever bit of bread, whatever I could buy.
Yes, there were ration cards.
And it was not adequate.
But we had means.
And certainly, who had still a little bit of money, or a ring, or a brooch, you could exchange.
And there were people who were bringing oil, flour, into the ghetto, illegally.
So it did exist.
But one had to pay for it dearly.
And we certainly have not been so well off.
We were very much middle class.
So we did not accumulate money, or jewelry, or anything like that.
My mother had sheets, pillowcases, towels.
And with that was good enough to procure some food.
At the beginning, it sort of-- non-- non-- event.
But later on, the SS used to come and just-- didn't like somebody, they would shoot them.
When there was [GERMAN], when there was a transport, who was not young enough, or not fit for work, they were shooting en masse.
And I also heard, like, when parents were discussing that people were being hanged in ghetto, and how they were saying, [NON-ENGLISH], at the last moment, at the gallows.
The people praying-- yes-- to God, in that-- in that terrible moment.
But, you know, my parents held everything in secret from the children, not to upset us.
And I think my parents must have been convinced that if they behave justly, if they will do what the Germans are telling us to do, we will survive.
The war will pass.
But that was just the beginning of most horrible era.
Because, later on, a whole transports of people were taken out of ghetto into Treblinka, into Majdanek.
There were people-- Poles-- aware that the people were taken there, shot, or they had to dig their own graves before they were shot, and then just shot in such a way that they fell into the grave.
And there were two, three Poles to cover this thing up.
So the stories trickled down to us.
But who wanted to believe the Germans would do this sort of thing for no reason, especially when they were telling us that they're taking them to village because-- all sorts of reasons?
The children were going to orphanage, it was told.
So some parents felt, well, they will be better off than in ghetto, where there is no food.
But somehow, nobody wanted to give their children.
Nobody wanted to go onto the transport.
But we were forced.
It was always SS, with guns.
And the resistance, slightest resistance, you were shot-- left in the street.
And that was a very common, later on, after the initial few weeks in ghetto.
Yes, I did.
I did.
[SIGHS] All of a sudden, lots of SS arrived in trucks into the ghetto, around the ghetto, with shouting.
Raus, raus, raus!
Everybody out the house!
And they went through the house, through the few-story flats or bricks houses.
[SIGHS] On the way, when they met somebody, bayoneting them-- really, sadistically-- it was-- run-- on the run, everybody on the run.
So we run wherever we were told to-- most people did.
Some managed to hide.
Some managed to avoid it.
But most of them were collected in the middle of the road, lots of SS around them, with their guns.
And the family, at that point, was so distraught that they only wanted to be together-- husband, wife, and the children-- that they shouldn't be separated.

And if somebody saw that did not get together with the family was hidden somewhere, will run out of that place where they were hidden to be together with the parents, or vice versa, or the wife or the husband.
We all try, at all times, to be together.
That was the strength.
That, if we are together, we will survive, wherever they're sending us.
I don't think so.
Nobody wanted to believe that.
Nobody wanted to believe that.
Yes.
We were always told that we're going to some factory, to some town, to do some work for the Germans.
Well, it was only fair.
If they are in charge and they need work, who are we to object to it?
And you will get food.

It was a very meager excuse for us, to get to-- to Majdanek or-- or-- we were very lucky, because in the house where we live, there was somebody who had certain information, or from outside, or knew somebody in Gestapo.
Because we knew before-- that I remember clearly at one stage, it was the ghetto to be liquidated-- to made smaller.
And they said that they're coming in the morning, and they will collect lots of Jews.
It was in thousands.
It was not just a few hundred or hundred-- a lots of Jews.
So my parents decided we're going before, out of the ghetto, and stay overnight.
And we will see what following day will bring.
Well, surely, following day, I was sent to my mother's friend, out of the ghetto, but in the path of the transport.
And at one stage, the lady in whose house I was staying-- she told me, I saw your parents going into the transport.
So you better go and join them.
But, of course, I knew better than that, because I knew where my parents were hidden.
They told us, you go to such-and-such a woman.
We going to this fellow.
My sister is going down there.
We distributed ourselves.
And I knew where they were.
They couldn't have been in a transport, because they were out of the ghetto.
But I didn't tell her.
And when she told me, you better go, I felt the situation is dangerous.
She's going to tell somebody that I'm hiding here.
She had sewing machine and few bits and pieces, and that was important for her, that I should not stay behind.
Because, God forbid, mother may ask for the sewing machine or something.
That was the attitude of Poles.
Yes-- and other things.
That was in the neighborhood.
See, we lived before the war in that area.
So that area was very known to me-- the streets, the people.
And I thought, right.
I'm not trusting you-- didn't take much not to trust.
It was such a poor excuse.
I said OK, bye-bye.
I run out of her house, and I went through the back streets where my father was hidden.
And they were furious.
Why did you leave there?
So I told them the story, why I left there-- or I could stay here.
And, believe it or not, it was a auto-- repair garage.
And my father was hidden there.
They told me to sit in some old car and not to come out, because the Germans were, in that particular garage, in charge, and, of course, were sure that my father is not Jewish.
He spoke fluently German.
He didn't look Jewish.
Except occasionally he would say to them, we have delivery to [NON-ENGLISH] which is a completely wrong word.
[NON-ENGLISH] meant [NON-ENGLISH], and it should have been [NON-ENGLISH].
So they thought a-- stupid Pole.
And that's how he went by, very often.
He would bring fish to ghetto.
So we had fish to eat, or something else.
So we managed to survive-- meagerly.
But we survive.
We ate.
And when there was to be-- [SIGHS] --transport, we were warned and dispersed the best we knew how.
And, occasionally, we couldn't get out.
My sister was caught once, pulled out from the cellar, and taken into the transport into Plaszow, beaten up completely, by the-- by the SS, by the soldiers.
But somebody spotted her and help her get out.
It was place called Plac Zgody, where we were congregating.
And there was an [NON-ENGLISH]/ And the [NON-ENGLISH], who was Pankiewicz--
Yes, pharmacy, that's right.
And what did I say?
[NON-ENGLISH] yes, it's pharmacy.
Thank you.
And at that stage, of course, we didn't know that he was collaborating with the Jews, because he was not Jewish.
And, with my young mind, I wouldn't have trusted him, either.
But my sister did, and she was hidden there and survived that particular transport.
Oh, he helped.
Later on, after the war, we have found out that he helped a lot of people.
He has really became a hero.
There are-- I have a few books.
He was a nice human being-- very, very nice-looking.
And, you know, some Poles realized that the war will end and they-- they decency was not completely destroyed.
They were not corrupted by the Germans.
But a lot of Poles were corrupted-- in particular, that they were allowed to come into our place when we moved first time.
And after the Germans finish with our place, they could take what they want.
So that was the attitude.
That-- that's how-- seemed to be-- everywhere.
Yes, there was.
No, because I had very dealing with anything or anybody.
My parents absolutely were hiding me.
You know, I was a big nobody-- really.
I was tall.
They were afraid that I will get caught.
And yet I was young.
So they were really protecting me very much.
I know that the head of that Judenrat was Spira, who could hardly speak Polish, or German, or whatever.
There were usually chosen people who were poorly educated.
They were power-hungry.
And he was one of those.
I remember seeing him walking in the ghetto with a great big cape around the uniform.
And he was exercising his little army.
But they were helpless when came to the Germans, because they were the first one to be shot.
After they finish they jobs, they were finished with.
That is the Jewish police I'm talking about.
That was called Judenrat.
In some places, there was Jewish council.
I think in Krakow was the police which had that power.
I read later, after the war, that there were rabbis and so on, and so.
But I had to really no contact with them.
My main duty was to bring food to ghetto to us-- to my family.
And we were also very fortunate that in the same house where we lived, there was a restaurant.
So when the curfew was-- which was always-- shops were closed, we would go through the corridor and go into that restaurant, and eat leftovers-- whatever was not sold.
But that was all at the beginning.
There all still-- there was still preserving a certain semblance of normality.
Yes, there was a school.
There was a-- a lady-- young woman, actually-- then, called Stefa Kempler.
And through ORT, we were sent to her.
And in her own home she was teaching us a little bit of English, a little bit of maths-- mostly sewing, which was of no interest to me.
But it was a pleasant atmosphere.
She created that warmth in the house.
One felt secure.
But that didn't take very long, because everybody had to go work.
And who didn't work, who didn't get the-- the working permit, was taken out of the ghetto.
And out of the ghetto meant to be collected-- go with the transport to wherever-- Majdanek, Belzec-- wherever they were shooting.
So everybody tried to work.
It was question-- a precisely life and death.
Because who didn't get the working permit, it meant death.
And I never did have the working permit.
I did work occasionally, but never secured the permit.
Because we were told that you can only work from that age on.
Up to certain age, you wouldn't qualify for work.
And we all had to carry a kennkarte with us, all had to have Ausweis to prove what age we are.

But occasionally, I managed to get on a truck-- because that's how we were collected-- to be taken to city to sweep the snow, or clean the German soldiers' barracks, or-- I remember once I went somewhere to clean the floors for a German officer to move in.
It was a lovely place.
And there was a little pantry.
And in the corner, there was an onion.
Now that was really a reward for all scrubbing all the floors in that place.
I came home, and I show my mother the onion I have found.
So my mother saw that I'm not as stupid as they thought.
They sort of allow me more often to go on those working excursions.
And most of the time they were working excursion.
Occasionally, you went on a truck, you didn't come back.
